105-
106-## Node.js runtime and browser support
107-
108-| Requirement   | Change / Details                                                   |
109-| ------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
110-| Node.js 20.9+ | Minimum version now `20.9.0` (LTS); Node.js 18 no longer supported |
111-| TypeScript 5+ | Minimum version now `5.1.0`                                        |
112-| Browsers      | Chrome 111+, Edge 111+, Firefox 111+, Safari 16.4+                 |
113-
114-## Turbopack by default
--
328:This approach gives you fully type-safe access to `props.params`, including the `slug`, and to `searchParams`, directly within your page.
329-
330-## Async parameters for icon, and open-graph Image (Breaking change)
331-
332-> The props passed to the image generating functions in `opengraph-image`, `twitter-image`, `icon`, and `apple-icon`, are now Promises.
333-
334-In previous versions, both the `Image` (image generation function), and the `generateImageMetadata` received a `params` object. The `id` returned by `generateImageMetadata` was passed as a string to the image generation function.
335-
336-```js filename="app/shop/[slug]/opengraph-image.js"
337-// Next.js 15 - synchronous params access
338-export function generateImageMetadata({ params }) {
339-  const { slug } = params
340-  return [{ id: '1' }, { id: '2' }]
341-}
342-
343-// Next.js 15 - synchronous params and id access
344-export default function Image({ params, id }) {
345-  const slug = params.slug
346-  const imageId = id // string
347-  // ...
348-}
349-```
350-
351-Starting with **Next.js 16**, to align with the [Async Request APIs](#async-request-apis-breaking-change) change, the image generating function now receives `params` and `id` as promises. The `generateImageMetadata` function continues to receive synchronous `params`.
352-
353-```js filename="app/shop/[slug]/opengraph-image.js"
354-export async function generateImageMetadata({ params }) {
355-  const { slug } = params
356-  return [{ id: '1' }, { id: '2' }]
357-}
358-
--
585:## Partial Pre-Rendering (PPR)
586-
587:**Next.js 16** removes the experimental **Partial Pre-Rendering (PPR)** flag and configuration options, including the route level segment `experimental_ppr`.
588-
589:Starting with **Next.js 16**, you can opt into PPR using the [`cacheComponents`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) configuration.
590-
591-```js filename="next.config.js"
592-/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */
593-const nextConfig = {
594-  cacheComponents: true,
595-}
596-
597-module.exports = nextConfig
598-```
599-
600:PPR in **Next.js 16** works differently than in **Next.js 15** canaries. If you are using PPR today, stay in the current Next.js 15 canary you are using. We will follow up with a guide to migrate to Cache Components.

text:
Now I have a clear understanding. In Next.js 16.2.0-canary.41, PPR is enabled through the `cacheComponents: true` config option instead of the old `experimental.ppr` flag. Let me also look at some more details about how PPR works with this new configuration.

Let me read the cacheComponents documentation page.

output:
1	---
2	title: cacheComponents
3	description: Learn how to enable the cacheComponents flag in Next.js.
4	---
5	
6	The `cacheComponents` flag is a feature in Next.js that causes data fetching operations in the App Router to be excluded from pre-renders unless they are explicitly cached. This can be useful for optimizing the performance of dynamic data fetching in Server Components.
7	
8	It is useful if your application requires fresh data fetching during runtime rather than serving from a pre-rendered cache.
9	
10	It is expected to be used in conjunction with [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) so that your data fetching happens at runtime by default unless you define specific parts of your application to be cached with `use cache` at the page, function, or component level.
11	
12	## Usage
13	
14	To enable the `cacheComponents` flag, set it to `true` in your `next.config.ts` file:
15	
16	```ts filename="next.config.ts"
17	import type { NextConfig } from 'next'
18	
19	const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
20	  cacheComponents: true,
21	}
22	
23	export default nextConfig
24	```
25	
26	When `cacheComponents` is enabled, you can use the following cache functions and configurations:
27	
28	- The [`use cache` directive](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache)
29	- The [`cacheLife` function](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheLife) with `use cache`
30	- The [`cacheTag` function](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag)
31	
32	## Notes
33	
34	- While `cacheComponents` can optimize performance by ensuring fresh data fetching during runtime, it may also introduce additional latency compared to serving pre-rendered content.
35	
36	## Version History
37	
38	| Version | Change                                                                                                                            |
39	| ------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
40	| 16.0.0  | `cacheComponents` introduced. This flag controls the `ppr`, `useCache`, and `dynamicIO` flags as a single, unified configuration. |
41

text:
Now I have a clear picture. In Next.js 16 (which this project uses, `16.2.0-canary.41`), Partial Pre-Rendering (PPR) is enabled through the `cacheComponents` config option. This replaces the old `experimental.ppr` flag.

The key changes in Next.js 16:
1. The `experimental.ppr` flag and route-level `experimental_ppr` export are removed
2. PPR is now enabled via `cacheComponents: true` in `next.config.ts`
3. When `cacheComponents` is enabled, data fetching is excluded from pre-renders unless explicitly cached with `use cache`
4. It's expected to be used with `use cache` directive to selectively cache parts of the application

The current app:
- `next.config.ts` has empty config
- `app/page.tsx` uses `<Suspense>` boundaries for `ProductList` and `Recommendations`
- Both `ProductList` and `Recommendations` fetch data from external APIs (dynamic data)

To enable PPR:
1. Add `cacheComponents: true` to `next.config.ts` — this enables PPR
2. With PPR enabled via `cacheComponents`, data fetching operations are excluded from pre-renders by default
3. The static shell (the `<h1>` and `<Suspense>` fallbacks) will be served immediately from the static prerender
4. The dynamic parts (ProductList, Recommendations) will be streamed in at runtime

Let me now implement this.

Now I have all the information I need. In Next.js 16 (which this project uses: `16.2.0-canary.41`), PPR has been simplified. The old `experimental.ppr` flag and `experimental_ppr` route segment config are gone — instead, PPR is enabled via the `cacheComponents` config option.

Let me enable it:

output: The file /home/user/workspace/next.config.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

text: Now let me verify the change looks correct by reading the file.

PPR is now enabled. Here's what this does for your app: ### What changed **`next.config.ts`** — added `cacheComponents: true` In Next.js 16, PPR has graduated from the old `experimental.ppr` flag to a single unified config: `cacheComponents: true`. This flag controls PPR, `use cache`, and `dynamicIO` together. ### How PPR works in your app now Your `app/page.tsx` already has the right structure for PPR: - **Static shell** — The `

Store

` heading and the `` fallbacks (`"Loading products..."`, `"Loading recommendations..."`) are pre-rendered at build time and served instantly. - **Dynamic holes** — `ProductList` and `Recommendations` (wrapped in ``) fetch their data at request time and stream in as it arrives, since `cacheComponents` excludes data fetching from pre-renders unless explicitly cached. This gives you the best of both worlds: the page shell loads immediately (static), while dynamic data streams in progressively (no waiting for all fetches to complete). ### Next step — opt into caching with `use cache` With PPR on, you can selectively cache parts of your app using the `use cache` directive. For example, if `ProductList` data doesn't need to be fresh on every request: ```ts export async function ProductList() { 'use cache' const products = await getProducts() // ... } ``` This tells Next.js to pre-render the component output at build time (making it part of the static shell), while `Recommendations` without the directive stays dynamic and streams in.
